nativeRange.habitat: Found primarily in rapidly flowing creeks and rivers with sandy substrates across South America, though it also occurs in slower reaches of large river systems.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on insect larvae, frozen or freeze-dried bloodworms, blackworms, brine shrimp, krill, and sinking pellets. Has been known to accept earthworms and small silversides. Tends to avoid dry flake foods. As a nocturnal feeder, it is best offered food after the lights are out for the evening.

avoidWith: other Black Ghost Knifefish (high conspecific aggression)
fast nippy fish like some rainbowfish

imageCandidates.notes: Vassil, 2262x1467. Striking full-body planted-tank shot; sharp, jet-black body, white tail-ring band, white snout blaze and long undulating anal fin all clearly visible.
Vassil, 1735x1019. Sharp horizontal side profile in planted aquarium; shows the ribbon anal fin and white tail band well.
Clinton & Charles Robertson, 1600x563. Clean sharp lateral documentary shot; diagnostic white tail ring with black tip and white snout crisply shown against a light background.

summary: The Black Ghost Knifefish (Apteronotus albifrons) is a nocturnal South American species that navigates using weak electrical pulses. It grows large and tends to be shy, requiring a spacious tank with caves, dim lighting, and tankmates too large to be prey.
careNotes: The Black Ghost Knifefish is a nocturnal, electroreceptive species that uses weak electrical pulses for navigation and communication; these pulses pose no known danger to humans or tankmates. Juveniles can start in a smaller tank but adults generally require at least 100 gallons (380 L) for long-term health; a 120-gallon (450 L) or larger footprint is commonly recommended for specimens reaching full size. Provide caves, PVC tubes, and driftwood as this species tends to be shy and stress-prone without adequate shelter. Floating plants and subdued or blue-spectrum lighting are appreciated. Generally peaceful toward fish too large to eat, but has been known to predate on smaller fish (under ~6 cm) as it matures; keep with same-sized or larger tankmates. Highly territorial toward conspecifics — generally best kept singly unless the tank is very large (200+ gallons) with visual barriers. Feed after lights out. This species is considered sensitive to declining water quality; maintaining good filtration and performing regular water changes is strongly recommended. Do NOT use copper-based medications — this species lacks true scales and is highly sensitive to copper. A tight-fitting lid is strongly recommended; this species has been documented jumping from open tanks (escape risk: high). Electrical organ navigation may be disrupted by electromagnetic interference from high-current devices near the tank; avoid placing multiple high-draw electrical devices directly against the tank wall.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely documented under typical home aquarium conditions. Eggs are reported to be laid in cave-like structures and are described as small and yellowish, hatching in roughly 3 days under favorable conditions. Parents have been known to consume eggs if not separated after spawning. No reliable captive breeding protocol is widely established; wild-caught individuals make up the majority of the trade.

additional.notes: Cross-checked size (max 50 cm), temperature (23–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), and hardness (5–19 dH). FishBase pH and gH widths used as they matched Fishkeeping World and superseded the narrower Aquarium Co-Op figures.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Confirmed copper sensitivity (explicit warning), nocturnal behavior, pH 6.0–8.0, gH 5–19 dGH, 100-gallon minimum, care level intermediate-to-advanced, and provided additional breeding details (cave spawning, ~3 day hatch). SeriouslyFish (first secondary) returned 404; Fishkeeping World used as replacement.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely documented. Eggs reported laid in caves, hatching in approximately 3 days; parents should be separated post-spawning to prevent egg predation. No established home aquarium breeding protocol exists.
